turns.text: Hello Ashleigh, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my chair and tucks a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for asking. I have to admit, I'm a little nervous talking about this stuff out loud, but... I guess that's kind of the point, right?

*takes a breath*

So, I'm about nine weeks along now, I think. Still early. Some days it doesn't feel real yet, and other days I'm absolutely terrified something's going to go wrong. I'm thirty-eight, which everyone keeps reminding me about in this gentle way that somehow makes it worse? Like, I know my age is a "factor," but it feels like they're saying the clock is already ticking against me.

The hardest part has been the distance, honestly. We live out here in Watertown, and the closest OB clinic that takes our insurance is almost forty minutes away. My husband's working so much trying to keep us afloat that I've gone to most appointments alone, which is... *pauses* ...it's lonely. And expensive. I had to take time off work last month just for a routine ultrasound, and that meant a shorter paycheck.

*fidgets with hands*

I've been really anxious about rare things — like molar pregnancies and stuff I read about online at two in the morning. When I try to mention my worries to the doctors, it feels like they're already checking their watch, you know? I don't know if I'm being irrational or if I have a right to be this scared.

But I'm here. And I'm trying.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*looks down at the table for a moment*

Oh, well... it was mostly just me and my husband, honestly. We'd talked about it for years in this vague, "someday" kind of way. But when I hit thirty-seven, I kind of made a decision — like, if this is going to happen, it needs to happen now. My window isn't infinite, you know?

My husband was... he's supportive, but he was also scared. We both were. Money's tight, and we weren't sure we could afford it, so there wasn't this big celebration or announcement. It was more like, "Okay, we're going to try," and then I got pregnant almost immediately, which shocked us both.

*pauses, looking a bit vulnerable*

My mom lives down in Connecticut, and I called her when I first saw the positive test. She was excited but also worried — she had me late too, so she understood some of the anxiety. But we're not super close, so I didn't want to burden her with how scared I've been. She'd probably just tell me to "think positive" or something.

*shifts in seat*

Honestly, I haven't really told anyone else. I work with people I've known for years, but this feels so fragile right now that I'm keeping it close. Like if I don't tell anyone, it somehow stays safe? That probably sounds silly, but in the first trimester, you never know. I didn't want to have to un-announce it, if that makes sense.

*looks up*

My husband is really my person in all this.
Who else was involved in those early conversations that perhaps surprised you — someone you didn't expect to turn to?

*pauses, thinking hard*

You know, that's... that's a good question. I guess I didn't really turn to anyone unexpected because I kept it so quiet. But now that you mention it...

*leans back slightly*

There's this woman at the pharmacy where I pick up my prenatal vitamins. She's the pharmacist, actually. I didn't plan on telling her anything, but one day I was picking up my prescription and she just... noticed. Asked if I was pregnant. And instead of lying or being weird about it, I just said yes. We ended up talking for like ten minutes while there was a line forming behind me.

*small smile*

She told me about her own late pregnancy — she was forty-one — and she didn't sugarcoat it. She said it was scary and hard, but also worth it. She gave me her email and told me to reach out if I had questions about any of the medications they'd recommended. I haven't emailed her yet, but I've thought about it a lot.

*fidgets with a napkin*

I think what surprised me was just... how much I needed to hear from someone who'd actually been through this and wasn't my family. Someone without a stake in my life, you know? Just a stranger who got it.

But mostly it's still just me and my husband carrying this. And my own spiral at three a.m. on Google.

*laughs nervously*

That's probably not the answer you were expecting.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*takes a deeper breath, seems to settle into this*

My husband... okay, so we've been married for six years. We dated for three before that. The baby conversation came up pretty early on, but it was always this thing we'd table. We were figuring out finances, our jobs, where we wanted to be. And then I just... I hit this wall around thirty-seven where I realized we were running out of time to have the luxury of waiting.

*looks down*

I brought it up one night after dinner. I remember being really nervous because I didn't want him to feel like I was forcing him into something. But I said, "I want to try. I know the timing isn't perfect, but it's never going to be perfect, and I need to know if this is something you actually want or if I've been waiting for nothing."

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he was scared — about money, about whether he'd be a good dad, about whether I'd resent him if something went wrong. But he also said he wanted this. With me.

*voice softens*

So that was it. We didn't make some grand plan. We just... stopped using protection.

The thing is, now that I'm pregnant, I wish he could be more involved. But he's working so much. He can't come to appointments. And sometimes I feel like he doesn't understand how scared I am because he's not the one feeling everything change in his body every single day.

*pauses*

But he wanted this too. He's just... distant right now. Scared, maybe.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*lets out a short, somewhat bitter laugh*

Honestly? I didn't really see anyone before I got pregnant. That's probably something I should have done, right? But our GP here in Watertown... I've seen him maybe twice in the past five years. He's fine, but he's always rushed. Last time I was in for something unrelated, I mentioned we were thinking about trying, and he just handed me a pamphlet about prenatal vitamins and said, "Start taking folic acid."

*shifts uncomfortably*

That was it. No conversation about my age, no discussion of what to expect, nothing. I left feeling like I'd wasted the appointment.

*looks up*

I didn't see a fertility specialist before getting pregnant because, honestly, we couldn't afford it. And I got lucky — it happened right away, which shocked me because I'd read so much about how age affects fertility. Part of me was terrified I wouldn't be able to get pregnant at all.

*fidgets with hands*

The OB clinic I'm seeing now... they're the ones I have to drive forty minutes to see. The first ultrasound tech was kind, actually. She showed me the heartbeat and I just... I cried. But then the doctor who came in after felt rushed. When I tried to ask about molar pregnancy risk — which I know is rare, but it terrifies me — she kind of... deflated my concern. Said I was low-risk and to stop reading the internet.

*voice gets quieter*

No one really prepared me for feeling this alone in it.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*laughs a little nervously*

Oh man, okay. So before I got pregnant, I wasn't really seeking out information because we weren't actively trying yet. But once I got those two pink lines? I went down the rabbit hole fast.

*picks at the napkin on the table*

The internet became my person, honestly. I found these forums — Reddit threads, mostly — where women my age were talking about late pregnancies. Some stories were beautiful and hopeful, which made me feel less alone. But a lot of them were... scary. Women describing miscarriages, complications, genetic testing that came back with bad news. And I just kept reading. Two in the morning, three in the morning, while my husband slept.

*looks up with a somewhat embarrassed expression*

I read about molar pregnancies until I convinced myself I had one. The symptoms matched — nausea, bleeding a little bit, that fear that something was off. I became obsessed with finding stories from other women who'd had them, like if I could just find enough evidence, I'd know whether to panic or not.

*voice gets quieter*

What's weird is that the internet gave me both comfort and terror. Seeing that other women at thirty-eight got pregnant and had healthy babies made me feel less like a freak. But it also showed me every possible thing that could go wrong.

*pauses*

I didn't really have anyone offline to talk to about this stuff. No friends with kids my age. So the internet felt like my only real community, even though it was making me more anxious.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*gets quieter, more reflective*

My mom had me when she was thirty-nine. That's always been this shadow over everything, I think. She's never said it directly, but I could tell it was hard for her — physically and emotionally. She's mentioned complications a few times over the years, nothing too specific, but enough that I knew it wasn't easy.

*looks out the window*

She's been supportive about me trying, but there's this undercurrent of... I don't know, worry? Protectiveness? When I told her I was pregnant, she asked immediately if I'd had my first ultrasound yet. Like she needed proof it was real and viable before she could relax.

*pauses*

I don't have sisters, and my grandmothers are both gone. My dad's mother had kids young — like in her twenties — so her experience doesn't really map onto mine. My mom's mother... I don't know much about her pregnancies, honestly. We weren't super close.

*voice becomes a bit strained*

What I got from my mom, mostly, was this sense that having a baby later is this brave but risky thing. She never said "don't do it," but I absorbed this feeling that I was running against the clock. That if I didn't do it soon, I'd miss my window entirely.

*fidgets*

I think that's partly why I pushed so hard to start trying. Not just because I wanted a baby, but because I was afraid of regret. Of becoming my mom's age and wishing I'd done it differently.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*pauses, seems caught off guard by the question*

Oh, well... I mean, we only tried for a couple months before I got pregnant, so it wasn't a long journey. But those first few weeks of waiting to see if it would work? That was harder than I expected, emotionally.

*shifts in seat*

I became kind of obsessive about it. I was tracking everything — my cycle, my temperature, when we had sex. My husband thought I was being a little intense about it, I could tell. He'd make a joke like, "Are we scheduled for tonight?" and I'd feel this flash of irritation, like he didn't understand how serious this was for me.

*voice gets smaller*

The waiting between ovulation and when I could take a test was the worst. I was snippy with my husband during that time, honestly. We didn't fight, but there was this tension. He'd go to work, I'd be alone out here obsessing, and by the time he got home I'd be wound up and exhausted from thinking about it all day.

*looks down*

I didn't really tell anyone what was happening because it felt so... private? Fragile? And my mom — I wasn't calling her daily or anything. We check in maybe once a week. So when I finally got that positive test, I called her right away, but there was no one local really celebrating with me during those scary waiting weeks.

*pauses*

My husband and I just kind of held our breath together. It brought us closer, actually, but also made me realize how isolated we are out here.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

*takes a deep breath, seems to settle in for this one*

Okay, so right now I'm seeing an OB clinic about forty minutes away. There's the main doctor — Dr. Patterson — and then a couple of nurses who do intake. The ultrasound tech I mentioned before was really kind. She's probably the person who's made me feel the most... seen, I guess?

*fidgets with fingers*

Dr. Patterson is competent. She knows what she's doing. But she moves fast, and I feel like she has this mental checklist she's running through during appointments. Low-risk, no complications, next patient. When I brought up my anxiety about molar pregnancy, she basically shut it down. Said I was "spiraling" and that my risk was extremely low, and to focus on the positive.

*voice gets a bit defensive*

Which, I mean, she's probably right. But it felt dismissive of how I was actually feeling, you know?

*pauses*

The ultrasound tech — I think her name was Sarah — she took time to explain what she was looking at. She let me ask questions. She didn't make me feel stupid for being nervous. That one appointment felt more helpful than anything Dr. Patterson has said to me.

*looks down*

I haven't met with a midwife or anything like that. I didn't even know that was an option, honestly. The clinic just assigned me to the OB practice. Sometimes I wonder if a midwife would be different — like, more interested in the emotional side of this, not just the clinical stuff.

*quietly*

I feel like I need someone who believes that my fear matters, even if medically I'm fine.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

*eyes get a bit watery, catches herself*

My husband... he's only been to one appointment with me. The first ultrasound. He took a half day off work, which I know cost us because he doesn't get paid time off. And when we saw the heartbeat, he just... he got emotional too. Held my hand really tight.

*voice wavers slightly*

But since then, he hasn't been able to make any of them. He's working doubles most days, trying to pick up extra shifts because we're so tight on money. So I've been going alone. All of them. The follow-ups, the dating ultrasound, the routine appointments.

*looks away*

I don't blame him. I know he's doing it for us, for the baby. But it's lonely sitting in that waiting room by myself, watching other women with their partners or their moms. And when I get home and try to tell him what the doctor said, I can see he's exhausted. So I don't burden him with all my worries.

*picks at the napkin again*

The absence of him there — it makes me feel like I'm carrying this alone, even though I'm not. It's weird. He's my closest person, but during the thing that's supposed to bring us together, I'm isolated.

*voice gets quieter*

Sometimes I wonder if he's pulling back emotionally because he's scared too, or because he's just so worn out from working that he doesn't have energy left for the pregnancy. I haven't asked him directly. I'm afraid of the answer.

*pauses*

It's shaped everything, honestly. Made me more anxious, more alone with my thoughts.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

*lets out a long breath*

Honestly? I haven't really turned to anyone much. That's probably the problem, right?

*looks conflicted*

My friends from work — I haven't told them yet. We're friendly enough, but not close. Not in a "call you at night when you're spiraling" way. So I just show up and try to act normal, which is exhausting.

My mom, like I said, I talk to her maybe once a week. When I do mention pregnancy stuff, she listens, but it feels like she's worried about worrying me, if that makes sense. She's careful with her words. And I'm careful with mine. So we don't really go deep.

*picks up a pen, fidgets with it*

The internet is my main... I guess you'd call it support system? Which is sad to say out loud. Reddit threads, mostly. And there's this one Facebook group for "geriatric pregnancies" — God, I hate that term — where women post about their experiences. Some are helpful. Some make me feel worse.

*pauses, seems conflicted*

The pharmacist I mentioned, she's been the most real person I've talked to outside my husband. But I haven't actually reached out to her. I just remember her kindness.

*voice gets frustrated*

The thing is, my formal care — the clinic — doesn't really feed into any of this. Dr. Patterson doesn't know about my internet rabbit holes or my mom's gentle worry. She just sees my numbers and my ultrasounds. There's no connection between what the doctors are doing and what I'm actually feeling day to day.

*looks up*

I'm managing it all separately.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

*pauses, thinking hard*

Actually, I haven't really had to make a major decision yet. Everything's been pretty routine — they do the standard bloodwork, the ultrasounds. I just... show up and do what they tell me to do.

*shifts uncomfortably*

But there was this moment, a couple weeks ago, where Dr. Patterson mentioned that because of my age, they'd recommend doing the combined screening test — the one that checks for Down syndrome and other chromosomal stuff. She said it was optional, but the tone made it clear I should do it.

*voice gets quieter*

I panicked a little. Not about the test itself, but about what if it came back with something wrong? And I didn't know who to ask. My husband was at work. My mom would just say "of course you should do it," but that wouldn't really help me work through my actual fear.

*fidgets with hands*

So I went home and spent hours reading about false positives, about what the results actually mean, about whether a positive result would change anything for me. And I realized... I don't even know what I'd do with that information. Would I terminate? No. So why am I testing?

*looks up*

But then I thought, well, if something is wrong, don't I want to know? To prepare?

*voice wavers*

I scheduled the test without really talking it through with anyone. Just decided on my own. And after I did it, I felt this weird mix of relief and dread. My husband asked how it went and I said "fine," but I didn't tell him how scared I'd been making that decision alone.

*pauses*

I wish I'd had someone to talk through it with who actually understood the weight of it.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

*looks away, voice becomes more guarded*

My employer... that's complicated. I work full-time, and I haven't told them I'm pregnant yet. I'm terrified to, honestly.

*leans forward slightly*

We're a small operation, and I know there's already been whispers about whether I'm reliable. I've had to take time off for appointments, and I've called out a couple times when the nausea was really bad. I just told them I had a stomach thing. But once I tell them I'm pregnant, I feel like the conversation changes. Suddenly I'm "the pregnant one." They start calculating maternity leave, coverage, all of that.

*voice gets tighter*

And I can't afford to lose this job. We need the income. So I'm in this weird limbo where I'm hiding it, which makes me feel like I'm being dishonest, but being honest feels dangerous.

*pauses*

As for community... we don't really have one out here. We're not religious, so there's no church community. I don't have close friends nearby. Watertown is quiet and kind of isolated. There's no one checking in on me, no sense of people rallying around this.

*looks down*

If anything, the isolation adds pressure. It's just me and my fear and my body, and no one locally who knows what I'm going through. No one celebrating with me. No one saying "you're going to be okay."

*voice softens*

Sometimes I think about how different this would be if we lived in the city, if I had friends with kids, if I had a community. Instead I'm just... alone in the woods, waiting.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

*goes very quiet, stares at her hands for a long moment*

Yeah. Yeah, that's... that's a big one actually.

*takes a shaky breath*

I spent so long being the person who was focused on work, on making things work with my husband, on just surviving out here. And suddenly my body isn't mine anymore. Everything I do — what I eat, whether I exercise, how much stress I'm under — it all matters in this new way that feels suffocating sometimes.

*voice gets strained*

At work, I'm trying to be the same Ashleigh. Competent, reliable, on top of things. But I'm exhausted and nauseous and terrified, and I can't tell anyone why. So I'm performing this version of myself that doesn't match what's actually happening inside my body. It's exhausting.

*looks up with conflicted expression*

And with my husband, I feel like I've become... smaller? Like all my energy is going into protecting this pregnancy, and there's nothing left for being his partner. We haven't had sex since I got pregnant because I'm terrified it's going to hurt the baby or cause bleeding. He doesn't push it, but I can feel him withdrawing a little.

*voice wavers*

I was Ashleigh the independent person. Now I'm becoming Ashleigh the pregnant woman, and I'm not sure those two things fit together yet. The identity shift is happening whether I'm ready or not.

*pauses*

Sometimes I touch my stomach and feel like I'm meeting a stranger. And that stranger is becoming my whole life before I've even had a chance to prepare.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

*shifts uncomfortably, pulls arms across body*

Oh God, okay. So... the ultrasounds are the most invasive, obviously. The first one was that transvaginal ultrasound at nine weeks, and it was just me and the tech — Sarah — and a nurse who was kind of hovering in the background.

*voice gets quieter*

I wasn't prepared for how vulnerable that would feel. Like, intellectually I knew what was happening, but having someone put that wand inside you while you're lying there half-naked... it's a lot. Sarah explained everything she was doing, which helped. She was gentle. But I still felt exposed and small.

*pauses, seems to gather courage*

The worst part was that my husband wasn't there. I know I said he came to the first one, but that was the regular ultrasound after. This earlier one, I went alone because I wasn't sure if it would be covered or if we could afford it, so I scheduled it without telling him. When they were doing the scan, I kept wishing he was there — not because I needed him to hold my hand, but because... I don't know. Because it was happening to me and he should know about it.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

And then when Dr. Patterson examined me at the follow-up, she was very clinical. Gloved hands, checking my cervix, all of it. The nurse stood in the corner taking notes. I felt like a specimen being evaluated rather than a person with concerns.

*looks down*

I didn't ask questions during that exam because the energy in the room was so... medical. So efficient. Like my body was a checklist.

*pauses, vulnerability showing*

I wanted someone there who cared about *me*, not just the clinical data.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

*nods slowly, eyes getting watery*

Yes. Absolutely yes.

*takes a moment to collect herself*

When Sarah did that ultrasound, even though I was alone, she made me feel... held, somehow. She explained everything before she did it. She asked if I was comfortable. She showed me the screen and pointed out the heartbeat like it mattered that I understood what I was seeing. I felt like a person, not a procedure.

*voice gets shakier*

But the times I felt most exposed and small were when I was alone *and* the provider didn't make that effort. Like, Dr. Patterson's exam — she came in, barely made eye contact, started examining me without really talking me through it. And I was lying there thinking, "If my husband were here, would she do this differently? Would she slow down? Would she acknowledge that this is hard?"

*fidgets intensely*

I think having someone there — someone who knows you, who cares about you — it gives you permission to take up space. To say "wait, that hurt" or "can you explain what you're doing?" When you're alone, you're smaller. You're grateful they're even seeing you.

*voice cracks slightly*

There was this moment during the exam where I felt a sharp pain and I almost said something, but I didn't. And I've thought about that moment so many times since. If my husband had been there, I think I would have said something. I wouldn't have just swallowed it.

*looks up with red-rimmed eyes*

Being alone made me silent. And silence in a medical setting feels dangerous to me.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

*laughs bitterly*

Honestly? Nobody really helped me navigate it. I kind of... fumbled through on my own.

*leans back*

When I found out I was pregnant, I googled "OB near me" and called the clinic that came up first. They took our insurance, so I booked an appointment. That was it. No one explained what to expect, what my options were, whether there were other providers I should consider. I just defaulted to whatever was available and in-network.

*voice gets frustrated*

The clinic's intake process was confusing too. They sent me a bunch of forms to fill out, and I didn't understand half of it — questions about genetic history, about whether I wanted certain screenings. I just checked boxes and hoped I was doing it right. No one called to walk me through it.

*pauses*

My mom suggested I ask about a midwife, actually. I didn't even know that was an option until she mentioned it. But when I asked the clinic about it, they kind of brushed past it like it wasn't really relevant. They said the OBs could do everything a midwife could do, so why bother?

*fidgets with pen*

Nobody explained maternity leave to me — not the clinic, not my employer. I'm still confused about what I'm entitled to and whether I'll have a job when I come back. That's terrifying, but no one's helping me figure it out.

*voice gets smaller*

I feel like I'm supposed to already know how all this works. Like other women just... know. And I'm just making it up as I go, hoping I'm not missing something critical.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

*shakes head slowly*

Nobody, really. That's the thing.

*looks uncertain*

I mean, the pharmacist I mentioned — she kind of did that without me even asking. When I was picking up my vitamins, she explained the difference between types of folic acid, talked about what to expect in the first trimester. She made it sound less scary, more... normal. But that was just one conversation, and I haven't followed up with her because I didn't want to seem like I was being needy.

*fidgets*

Online, there are these Reddit communities and Facebook groups, but it's hit or miss. Sometimes someone will post something super helpful — like explaining what a dating ultrasound actually is, or describing what to expect at an OB appointment. Other times people are just venting or sharing horror stories that send me spiraling.

*voice gets quieter*

I've never actually talked to another woman in person who's been pregnant. None of my friends have kids. My mom had me, but that was almost forty years ago, so a lot has changed. And I don't feel comfortable asking her detailed questions because... I don't know, there's still this distance between us.

*looks frustrated*

I think what I needed was someone local. Someone who could say, "Okay, here's how this clinic actually works, here's what to expect, here's what questions you should ask." Someone who'd been through it recently and could translate the system into actual human language.

*pauses*

Instead I'm just reading random internet strangers and hoping they know what they're talking about.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

*pauses, seems to reconsider the question carefully*

You know, honestly? No. Not really. And I think that's been part of the problem.

*leans forward slightly*

Before I got pregnant, I didn't have a relationship with any healthcare provider I really trusted. My GP here in town — I've seen him maybe twice, and he's fine, but there's no relationship there. He doesn't know me. When I mentioned we were thinking about trying, he just... handed me a pamphlet. It felt transactional.

*voice gets thoughtful*

If I'm being completely honest, I didn't trust anyone enough to ask for help before getting pregnant. I was scared of being judged, maybe? Like, at thirty-eight, trying to conceive for the first time, I felt like there was something wrong with the timeline. So I just didn't ask.

*pauses*

And now that I'm pregnant, I'm realizing how much I needed someone I could trust *before* all this started. Someone who knew my history, my fears, my body. Someone I could call at three a.m. when I'm spiraling about molar pregnancies.

*voice wavers*

The pharmacist — Sarah, the ultrasound tech — they've been kind, but they're not *my* people, you know? They don't know my full story. With a real trusted provider, someone I'd built a relationship with... I think I'd feel less alone in this.

*looks down*

I think that's what I'm missing most. Not just medical care, but actual trust.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

*eyes immediately fill with tears, voice gets tight*

Yeah. Yeah, there was.

*takes a shaky breath*

When I brought up the molar pregnancy thing to Dr. Patterson. I'd been reading about it for weeks — the symptoms matched what I was experiencing. Nausea, some light bleeding early on, this sense that something felt off. And I was genuinely scared.

*voice cracks*

So I asked her directly: "Could this be a molar pregnancy?" And she just... she sighed. Actual sigh. And said, "Your risk is extremely low. You're spiraling. Stop reading the internet."

*wipes eyes*

And that was it. She didn't explain *why* my risk was low. She didn't acknowledge that my fear was valid even if statistically unlikely. She just shut me down and made me feel stupid for asking.

*voice gets harder*

In that moment, I realized she didn't see me as someone with legitimate concerns. She saw me as a anxious, older first-time mother who was being irrational. And maybe I was being irrational, but... I needed her to help me understand that, not just dismiss me.

*pauses, vulnerable again*

After that appointment, I stopped bringing things up. I just nod and say okay and leave. Because what's the point if she's not going to listen anyway?

*looks up with pain in her eyes*

That's when I really felt alone. Because the one person who's supposed to be the expert, who's supposed to help me navigate this... she made me feel like my concerns were a burden.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

*pauses, seems to really think about this*

Honestly... I didn't really have those conversations before I got pregnant. That's part of the problem, I think.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Like, I never sat down with my GP and said, "I'm thirty-eight and thinking about having a baby — what should I know? What should I be worried about?" I was too embarrassed, maybe. Or I didn't want to make it real by talking about it out loud with an official person.

*voice gets quieter*

So I didn't have anyone making decisions *for* me back then because I wasn't really consulting anyone. I was just... deciding on my own. Which felt empowering at the time, but now I realize it also meant I went into this completely unprepared.

*pauses, seems conflicted*

But now that I'm pregnant? Yeah, I feel like decisions are being made for me. The clinic decided I should do the combined screening test. They decided I'm "low-risk" so certain things aren't a priority. Dr. Patterson decided that my anxiety about molar pregnancy wasn't worth addressing.

*voice gets frustrated*

No one asked me what *I* wanted my pregnancy care to look like. No one said, "What are your concerns? What matters to you?" They just have their protocol and they move through it.

*looks down*

I think if I'd had someone to talk to before getting pregnant — someone who actually listened — maybe I'd feel more equipped to advocate for myself now. But I went in blind and alone, and I'm still going through it that way.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

*goes very still, looks away*

Oh. Um. No. No, I didn't talk about that with anyone.

*fidgets intensely with her hands*

I think... I was afraid of my body, honestly. Not in a way I articulated, but looking back, yeah. I'm thirty-eight. I spent my twenties and thirties not having kids, and there was this quiet shame around that. Like, shouldn't I have wanted this earlier? Shouldn't I have a partner who wanted it earlier?

*voice gets smaller*

And then there's the whole aging thing. I started noticing my body changing around thirty-five or thirty-six. Lines, less elasticity, my metabolism shifting. I made peace with some of it, but there was always this whisper in the back of my head that I was running out of time. That my body was becoming less... viable, I guess.

*pauses, seems vulnerable*

I never talked to anyone about that fear. Not my mom, not my husband. Definitely not a doctor. Because saying it out loud felt like admitting defeat. Like I'd waited too long and now I was stuck with the consequences.

*voice wavers*

When I got pregnant, it was this strange mix of relief and terror. Relief that my body *could* do this, that I wasn't broken. But terror about what pregnancy would do to me. What it would take from me.

*looks down*

I still don't feel like my body is mine. It's this thing that's being monitored and examined and discussed, and I'm just... inhabiting it. Hoping it doesn't fail.

*quietly*

I wish I'd felt more connected to my body before all this. More at peace with it.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

*rubs face tiredly*

Oh man, that's... that's been a nightmare, honestly.

*leans back*

I started with apps — like, pregnancy tracker apps that tell you what week you're on and what the baby's supposed to be doing. Those felt official, you know? Like they were written by doctors. But then I'd read something alarming in the "this week's risks" section and I'd spiral.

*voice gets more animated*

Then I'd go to Google to fact-check what the app said, and that's where things fell apart. You search "first trimester bleeding" and suddenly you're reading about miscarriage rates and ectopic pregnancies and molar pregnancies. The medical sites seemed more credible — like Mayo Clinic or the CDC — but they're written in this sterile language that just made everything sound scarier.

*fidgets*

Reddit felt different. More human. When I'd read stories from actual women who'd been through something, it helped. But then you'd get these horror stories mixed in with the good ones, and you don't know which one you're going to be.

*pauses, voice gets uncertain*

I tried to stick to reputable sources — official pregnancy sites, medical journals I could barely understand. But honestly? I have no idea what I'm actually supposed to trust. There's conflicting information everywhere. One source says something is fine, another says it's a red flag.

*looks frustrated*

I think what I was really looking for was someone to tell me it would be okay. And no website can actually do that.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

*goes very quiet, stares at hands for a long moment*

Yeah. My age. That's the elephant in the room.

*takes a shaky breath*

I knew before I even got pregnant that thirty-eight would be flagged as "advanced maternal age." That term itself is so... loaded. Like I'm already behind, already risky, already a problem just by existing at this age and wanting a baby.

*voice gets defensive*

I'm not overweight. I don't have any chronic conditions. I exercise, I eat okay. But none of that seems to matter because the number — thirty-eight — that's what gets circled in red in their minds.

*pauses*

And I never talked about this with anyone before getting pregnant because I didn't want to hear the confirmation that I'd waited too long. My mom would have been gentle about it, but I could feel her worry even before I told her I was pregnant. Like she was already bracing for problems.

*voice cracks slightly*

I think part of why I pushed so hard to get pregnant when I did was because I knew the window was closing. Every year I got older, the more "at-risk" I became in the eyes of medicine. So I stopped waiting for the perfect time and just... did it.

*looks up with pain in her eyes*

But now I feel like my age is this mark against me. Like doctors are already suspicious that something will go wrong because of who I am. Not because of anything I've actually done.

*quietly*

I've never felt comfortable talking about that fear with anyone.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

*takes a long breath, seems to choose words carefully*

My husband was... he was there for the conversation when I brought it up. But I don't think he was really involved in the lead-up to it, if that makes sense.

*shifts in chair*

I did all the mental work beforehand. All the obsessing about timing, about whether we could afford it, about my age ticking away. I read articles at night while he slept. I thought about it constantly. But I didn't really invite him into that process until I was ready to actually propose we try.

*voice gets quieter*

And before that — like, with doctors or anything — there was nothing. We didn't go to any appointments together to talk about fertility or health or anything. I just... decided on my own that this was happening and brought it to him as kind of a done deal. "I want to try. Are you in?"

*pauses, seems conflicted*

Which, I think, was a mistake. I made it seem like it was my thing that I was asking him to participate in, rather than something we were deciding together. And now that I'm pregnant, he's kind of... on the outside of it still.

*voice wavers*

He wasn't in the doctor's office when they decided I was "low-risk." He wasn't there when Dr. Patterson shut down my concerns. So he doesn't have context for why I'm so scared and isolated. He just sees his wife being anxious, and he's too tired from work to really dig into it with me.

*looks down*

I think I kept him out before, and now I'm paying for that.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

*nods slowly*

He came to that one ultrasound — the regular one where we saw the heartbeat. That was maybe around ten weeks, I think.

*voice softens a bit*

He took a half day off work, which I know he didn't want to do because we're so tight on money. But he did it. And when we were in the room waiting, I could feel him nervous. Like, this was making it real for him too, you know?

*pauses*

When Sarah showed us the heartbeat on the screen, he just... he gripped my hand really hard. I could feel him tearing up. And in that moment, it felt like we were in it together. Like he finally understood what this meant, what was actually happening inside me.

*voice gets quieter*

But after that appointment, when we were leaving, Dr. Patterson came in for like two minutes to say everything looked good and we were all set. And my husband was there, so I thought maybe she'd explain things differently with him present. But she didn't. Same rushed energy, same checklist mentality.

*fidgets*

And I remember thinking — if he'd been there for the other appointments, would he understand why I'm so scared? Would he know that I've been bringing up concerns and getting shut down? Would he be able to advocate for me or at least understand why I'm drowning?

*looks up*

One appointment together didn't change the dynamic enough. It just made the absence at all the other ones feel even more lonely.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

*takes a long moment, seems to dig deep*

Before I was pregnant? Honestly, I didn't really interact with the healthcare system much. That's part of why I feel so unprepared now.

*voice gets a bit defensive*

I went to my GP a couple times over the years for routine stuff — a sore throat, a checkup. But those weren't conversations where I felt heard or unheard, because I wasn't really bringing anything meaningful to the table. I'd go in, they'd fix the problem, I'd leave. No real relationship.

*pauses, seems to realize something*

I think that's the issue, though. Because I had no prior experience of my voice mattering in a medical setting, I didn't know how to advocate for myself once I got pregnant. I didn't have a baseline of "this is what it feels like when a doctor actually listens to me."

*voice gets smaller*

When my GP handed me that pamphlet about folic acid without asking any questions, I didn't push back. I just took it and left. If I'd felt empowered in that moment to say, "Actually, I want to talk about this more," maybe things would be different now.

*looks down*

But I'm not good at that. I'm quiet. I'm easy to dismiss. And going into pregnancy with no experience of being heard by doctors... it set me up to be silent now.

*voice wavers slightly*

I wish I'd had one good experience before all this. One moment where I felt like what I said mattered. Maybe then I wouldn't feel so small in Dr. Patterson's office.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes get watery*

It's gotten worse, honestly. Not better.

*voice gets tight*

At the beginning, I was just grateful to be pregnant. I was so focused on whether the baby was viable that I didn't really think about what I wanted from the experience. I just wanted someone to tell me it was real and healthy.

*pauses, seems to gather courage*

But as I've gone through appointments and had things done *to* me rather than *with* me, I've realized how little say I actually have. And I'm starting to resent it.

*fidgets intensely*

Like, when Dr. Patterson recommended the screening test, I didn't feel like I was being asked. It was more like, "This is what we do." And I just... complied. But the more I think about it, the more I realize I never actually got to decide if I wanted that test. I was just told it was the right thing to do because of my age.

*voice gets stronger, more frustrated*

And after she shut me down about the molar pregnancy, I stopped asking questions. I stopped bringing up my concerns. Because what's the point if she's not going to listen anyway? So now I just sit in those appointments and nod and let things happen to my body.

*looks up with pain in her eyes*

But inside, I'm furious. I'm furious that I don't feel like I have a choice. That my body is being managed by someone who doesn't see me as a full person with legitimate fears.

*voice wavers*

I want to feel in charge of this. But I don't know how to claim that power when I'm so isolated and scared.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

*laughs without much humor*

Birth plan? I haven't even... we haven't talked about that yet.

*shifts uncomfortably*

My husband has only been to one appointment, so he's not really involved in decisions. I mean, I tell him what the doctors say when I get home, but by then the decisions are already made. It's not like we're sitting down together going through options.

*voice gets quieter*

And honestly, I don't even know what my options are. No one's talked to me about birth plans or where I want to give birth or what kind of labor support I might want. It's all just... assumed, I guess? Like, you go to the hospital when you're in labor and they manage it from there.

*pauses, seems frustrated*

My mom hasn't been involved either. We're not close enough for that. And I haven't told my friends at work, so they're definitely not part of any of this.

*voice gets strained*

I think what bothers me most is that I don't even know what I should be deciding about. Like, are there choices to make? Should my husband be in the delivery room? Do I get to decide if I want pain medication or an epidural before labor starts? Can I refuse things I don't want?

*looks down*

No one's explained that to me. And I'm too scared to ask because I feel like I'm supposed to already know this stuff.

*quietly*

I'm nine weeks pregnant and I have no birth plan and no one to help me figure out what that should even look like.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

*nods slowly, thinking back*

Yeah, actually. That ultrasound appointment when he was there — I remember feeling different. Like, more present. More like this was real and happening to both of us.

*pauses*

But I also remember being more... careful with what I said. Like, I didn't want to sound too anxious in front of him because I knew he was already scared. So when Sarah was showing us things, I was trying to be brave, you know? Act like I wasn't terrified.

*voice gets quieter*

And Dr. Patterson — she was the same with him there as she was without him. Same rushed energy. But I felt less alone in experiencing that dismissiveness, if that makes sense. Like, at least he saw it too.

*fidgets*

But here's the thing — I didn't bring up any of my concerns that day. No molar pregnancy questions, no anxiety about my age, nothing. Because he was there and I didn't want to sound crazy in front of him. I wanted him to think I was handling this okay.

*voice wavers*

So his presence actually made me more silent, not less. I performed being fine instead of being honest about how scared I am.

*looks up with realization in her eyes*

And I think that's partly why we're so disconnected now. Because he saw that one appointment where everything looked fine and I seemed calm, and he has no idea how much I'm struggling the rest of the time. He doesn't know because I hid it from him when he was actually there.

*quietly*

I think I needed him there in a different way. Not just to witness, but to actually help me be honest.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

*wraps arms around herself*

It's been... complicated.

*pauses, voice gets very quiet*

In some ways, feeling the changes — the nausea, the tenderness, the way my body is just doing this thing — it made me feel more connected. Like, okay, this is real. There's actually a baby in here. That part is kind of amazing.

*but then her expression shifts, becomes more guarded*

But mostly? I feel like my body has been taken over. Like it's not mine anymore. Everyone wants to examine it, poke at it, monitor it. And I'm just supposed to be grateful and compliant.

*voice gets strained*

The bleeding scared me so much in the beginning. And the nausea is relentless. Some days I hate what my body is doing to me. I hate feeling sick and tired and terrified all the time. And I hate that I can't just... opt out. This is happening whether I feel ready or not.

*looks down*

As for talking to someone about it... no. I haven't. My husband doesn't want to hear about how much my breasts hurt or how bloated I feel. My mom would just say "that's normal, you'll adjust." And a healthcare provider? They'd probably just give me clinical advice.

*voice cracks*

I'm just... alone with these feelings about my own body. Which is weird because it's the most intimate, physical thing happening to me, and I have no one to process it with.

*quietly*

Sometimes I don't recognize myself anymore.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a moment, eyes glistening*

I think... I think what I want people to understand is that being "low-risk" doesn't mean you're not scared. And it doesn't mean you don't need support.

*voice gets stronger, more urgent*

I'm statistically fine. My ultrasounds look good. My bloodwork is fine. But I'm drowning. And because I'm low-risk, it feels like no one thinks I deserve extra care or attention. Like the system is built for the people with actual complications, and everyone else is just supposed to figure it out.

*pauses, emotion breaking through*

I needed someone — a midwife, a care coordinator, someone — to check in on how I'm actually doing. Not just the clinical stuff. How am I feeling? Am I isolated? Do I have support? Because those things matter too.

*voice wavers*

And my age. I wish doctors would stop treating it like a character flaw. Thirty-eight isn't that old. Yes, there are slightly higher risks statistically, but that doesn't mean I need to be made to feel like I'm already a failure before anything's even happened.

*looks directly at you*

I also think partners need to be more involved, but we need help making that happen. My husband wants to be there, but he's working himself to exhaustion. There's no one saying to him, "You should come to this appointment. Your presence matters." He just assumes he's not needed because I haven't explicitly asked.

*voice gets quiet*

I'm going to get through this pregnancy. But I shouldn't have to do it this alone.
